Title: Transposons played a major role in the diversification between the closely related almond and peach genomes: results from the almond genome sequence

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cytochrome P450-catalyzed early step in amygdalin biosynthesis; reported as one of the enzymes catalyzing the first steps of the pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Seed bitterness and toxicity; key domestication-related defense-associated metabolite in Prunus seeds
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seed/kernel, especially seed tegument context discussed
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms early pathway intermediate/required for amygdalin biosynthetic flux
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; reduced expression is correlated with sweet kernel phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced CYP71AN24 expression is correlated with reduced amygdalin accumulation and the sweet almond kernel phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP71AN24 is highly conserved between almond and related Prunus species at the sequence level. 2. In almond, almond-specific TE insertions, including two MITEs in the proximal upstream region, flank CYP71AN24 and are highly methylated. 3. Presence of these TE insertions, especially TIR2, correlates with sweet versus bitter seed phenotype, supporting reduced CYP71AN24 expression as a cause of low amygdalin/sweet kernel.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Texas; Cristomorto; Marcona; D05-187; S3067; Prunus webbii compared for locus structure
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Reduced expression in sweet almond varieties versus bitter almond varieties was cited from prior work; seed tegument context discussed
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low expression associated with sweet kernel phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Sweet versus bitter genetic background; TE insertion and DNA methylation state
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Highly methylated almond-specific TE insertions flank the gene; methylation near the locus is proposed to spread and contribute to reduced CYP71AN24 expression. Gene methylation is higher in almond than peach.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native promoter/proximal upstream region affected by TE insertions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomic analysis of orthologous loci; resequencing-based genotype-phenotype correlation across sweet and bitter accessions; DNA methylation profiling; literature-supported expression association
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing: ENA PRJEB32994; resequencing: ENA PRJEB32985, SRA SRX245830, SRX245832; bisulfite sequencing reported, accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value marker/candidate for sweet-kernel breeding. Selection against high-expression CYP71AN24 alleles or editing/removal of activating cis-elements could reduce amygdalin. Deployment should consider linked TE/methylation states and possible interaction with bHLH2 genotype.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reducing amygdalin may weaken chemical defense and alter bitterness/toxicity-related protection.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes cis-regulatory TE insertion/methylation around CYP71AN24 rather than coding-sequence change. A recently reported bHLH2 mutation is associated with sweetness, but the association is imperfect; Texas lacks the reported mutation, supporting additional regulation at CYP71AN24.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cytochrome P450 enzyme proposed to act in early amygdalin biosynthesis together with CYP71AN24.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for pathway flux toward amygdalin
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Lack of CYP79D16 expression has been proposed, together with reduced CYP71AN24 expression, to contribute to sweet kernel trait.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP79D16 is cited as another early amygdalin-pathway P450. 2. Lack of expression together with reduced CYP71AN24 expression has been proposed to underlie the sweet kernel trait. 3. This paper does not experimentally dissect CYP79D16 in detail.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Lack of expression in sweet kernel context cited from prior work
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Prior expression association cited; no direct new functional validation in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential secondary target for reducing amygdalin, but this paper provides limited direct evidence.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible reduction of cyanogenic defense compounds.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the paper explicitly discusses it with CYP71AN24 as contributing to sweet kernel, but it is not the main experimentally characterized locus here.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Binds the CYP71AN24 promoter; sweet-associated point mutation renders it unable to bind, implying loss of activation of CYP71AN24.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation (inferred from promoter binding requirement)
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss-of-binding bHLH2 allele is associated with the sweet almond phenotype via failure to activate CYP71AN24.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Conditionally important regulator of amygdalin accumulation/sweetness, but not sufficient to explain all sweet phenotypes because association is imperfect in this paper.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A published point mutation in bHLH2 was reported to abolish binding to the CYP71AN24 promoter and associate with sweet kernel phenotype. 2. This study finds the mutation absent in Texas despite sweet phenotype, and present in P. webbii despite bitter phenotype context noted by the authors. 3. Therefore bHLH2 association is not perfect, suggesting additional regulatory mechanisms such as TE-mediated silencing at CYP71AN24.
Regulation_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Texas and other sweet/bitter accessions compared
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Texas; Atocha mentioned from literature; other sweet and bitter accessions
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genotype analysis in this paper; promoter-binding defect association cited from Sanchez-Perez et al. 2019
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing: ENA PRJEB32994; resequencing ENA PRJEB32985 and cited external datasets
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful but incomplete marker for sweet-kernel breeding; should be combined with CYP71AN24-linked TE/methylation markers rather than used alone.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May have incomplete predictive power for sweetness; possible pleiotropy not assessed.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This paper treats bHLH2 mainly as an external comparative explanation and highlights exceptions that argue for additional cis-epigenetic regulation.
